comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes a Twin Neural Network (Twin NN) motivated by the Twin Support Vector Machine (TWSVM) to address classification on large, imbalanced datasets. The authors replace the matrix-inversion-based TWSVM dual formulation with a neural-network-based training procedure that trains two class-specific hyperplanes in learned feature space. Experiments on UCI and additional artificially generated highly imbalanced datasets report improvements in several metrics (accuracy, G-mean, F-measure, MCC) and training-time scalability comparisons versus SVM, Twin SVM and a regularized feed-forward NN.

Strengths:
- Addresses an important and practically relevant problem: scalable learning for highly imbalanced data.
- The idea of combining the twin-hyperplane intuition with a learned feature map is reasonable and could be useful in practice.
- The experimental evaluation uses a range of classical imbalance metrics (G-mean, F-measure, MCC) and reports statistical tests.

Major weaknesses and issues to address (required before stronger endorsement):
1) Mathematical clarity and correctness: key derivations are unclear and possibly incorrect. The loss definitions (Eqns. 11-12) and resulting gradients (Eqns. 13-16) contain multiple inconsistencies and likely typographical errors (e.g., many gradients reference φ(x^(i)(−1)) even when differentiating E(+1); the factor (1 - y^2) appears without defining the activation or output range; t(i) and y(i) are not precisely defined in the network context). The authors must provide a clean, consistent derivation: define network architecture, activation functions, output encoding, loss function per-sample, and show how the class-specific regularization terms arise. Also indicate whether updates are done by closed-form or by gradient-based optimization.

2) Inconsistent prediction rules: Eqn. (8) uses direct hyperplane distances while Eqn. (17) normalizes by ||w||. The reasoning for the normalization and its consistency with training objectives must be clarified.

3) VC-dimension arguments are hand-wavy and not well justified for neural networks. Theorem 1 is stated without proof nor precise assumptions; invoking classical VC bounds to claim reduced total risk for this NN architecture is misleading unless detailed assumptions and derivations are provided. Please either substantially strengthen the theoretical justification or remove/soften the claims.

4) Experimental detail and reproducibility: many important details are missing — exact network architectures (hidden layer sizes reported as tuned but not listed per dataset), activation functions, optimizer (SGD/Adam), learning rates, batch sizes, number of epochs, stopping criteria, initialization, regularization details, and how hyperparameter grid-search was conducted (ranges, validation folds). Without these, experiments are difficult to reproduce and results hard to trust.

5) Comparisons to relevant baselines: the paper compares against SVM, Twin SVM and a regularized NN, but omits widely used alternatives for imbalanced learning and scalable classifiers: cost-sensitive losses, class-weighting, oversampling/SMOTE, focal loss, gradient-boosted trees, and scalable solvers or approximations for TWSVM. A stronger empirical section should include some of these and/or justify why they were not included.

6) Statistical claims need care: the text sometimes misinterprets p-values (at one point it says p-values less than 0.05 do not indicate significant statistical difference — this is incorrect). Provide the full details of statistical tests (which test, paired/unpaired, exact p-values, how ties handled) and report confidence intervals or standard deviations for metrics across runs/folds.

7) Artificial generation of ‘‘highly unbalanced’’ datasets: converting multi-class datasets into one-vs-rest variants creates extreme imbalance in a mechanical way. While this is a legitimate way to study imbalance, authors should discuss how this differs from naturally imbalanced real-world data, and ideally include at least one real large-scale imbalanced benchmark (if available) or synthetic datasets with controlled imbalance properties.

8) Presentation issues and typos: several repeated paragraphs, duplicated sentences and layout artifacts from the preprint make the manuscript harder to follow. Equations and variable notations should be consistently defined. Tables and figures are referenced but not visible in the provided text — ensure the final submission includes clear tables, confidence intervals, and crucially confusion matrices for representative datasets.

Suggestions for improvement / additional experiments:
- Provide corrected, self-contained derivations of the Twin NN objective and gradients. State whether training is end-to-end with backpropagation and whether both networks share the hidden representation or have separate feature maps.
- Include ablation studies: (a) effect of class-specific feature maps vs shared map, (b) effect of the class-specific regularizer terms, (c) influence of network depth/width and activation choice, (d) comparison with class-weighted loss and oversampling methods.
- Add runtime complexity analysis (training time vs dataset size) with both asymptotic reasoning and measured wall-clock times, including hardware details.
- Release code and trained models to aid reproducibility.

Overall recommendation:
The paper tackles a worthwhile problem and contains a plausible idea, but in its current form has significant clarity and correctness issues in the derivation and lacks sufficient experimental detail and broader baselines. I encourage revision: fix the mathematical presentation, expand the empirical comparisons, and improve reproducibility. After these revisions the work could be a useful contribution for practitioners dealing with large, imbalanced datasets.
